title: Genomic Epidemiology of Influenza A: Tracking Viral Spread from Avian Flyways to Human Communities
authors_full: Matthew Scotch¹˒²; Temitope OC Faleye²; Angelica Urquidez-Negrete²; Bradley Bobbett²; Veronica Boyle²; Kelly Conard²; Lucy Sublasky-Rodriguez³; Vel Murugan²; Arvind Varsani²; Ann Fan⁴; Anne Justice-Allen⁴

summary.hook: This poster uses genomic surveillance to trace influenza A spread from avian flyways into human communities, with a focus on evidence that a Central flyway can act as a secondary...
summary.summary_30s: This poster uses genomic surveillance to trace influenza A spread from avian flyways into human communities, with a focus on evidence that a Central flyway can act as a secondary hub after introductions from the Pacific.

key_numbers: Timeline shown spanning Nov–Dec 2025; cryptic circulation inferred 6–16 months (for H5N1 D1.1).
Decisive Bayes factor support for pairwise flyway transmission shown as 20,243 for Pacific→Central and 20,243 for...
Median evolutionary rate for H5 HA estimated at 7.93×10⁻³ substitutions/site/year.
H5N1 D1.1 likely circulated undetected for 6–16 months (cryptic transmission).
Antigenic drift: 10 subclade K HA substitutions in RBD/antigenic sites; epitope modeling predicts low VE vs vaccine...
takeaways: Flyways & spread: The Central flyway is described as a secondary hub, initially a sink for introductions from the Pacific, then amplifying viral diversity and driving...
Transmission support: “Decisive Bayes factor support for pairwise transmission” is shown, with values 20,243 for Pacific→Central and 20,243 for Central→Mississippi.
Campus subclade expansion: “Rapid expansion of Subclade K on campus” with a spike in influenza diagnoses at Student Health in November 2025 (date callout 8 November...
